elements.sent-2411-001: We discuss Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) and event-chain Monte Carlo (ECMC) for the one-dimensional chain of particles with harmonic interactions and benchmark them against local reversible Metropolis algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-002: While HMC achieves considerable speedup with respect to local reversible Monte Carlo algorithms, its autocorrelation functions of global observables such as the structure factor have slower scaling with system size than for ECMC, a lifted non-reversible Markov chain.
elements.sent-2411-003: This can be traced to the dependence of ECMC on a parameter of the harmonic energy, the equilibrium distance, which drops out when energy differences or gradients are evaluated.
elements.sent-2411-004: We review the recent literature and provide pseudocodes and Python programs.
elements.sent-2411-005: We finally discuss related models and generalizations beyond one-dimensional particle systems.
elements.sent-2411-006: Markov-chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) permeates all fields of science, from physics to statistics and to the social disciplines.
elements.sent-2411-007: Reversible Markov chains, the great majority of MCMC methods, map the sampling of probability distributions onto the simulation of fictitious physical systems in thermal equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-008: In physics, thermal equilibrium is characterized by time-reversal invariance and the detailed-balance condition.
elements.sent-2411-009: It thus comes as no surprise that reversible Markov chains, such as the famous Metropolis~\cite{Metropolis1953 and heat-bath algorithms~\cite{Glauber1963,Creutz1980HeatBath,Geman1984 , all satisfy detailed balance.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: In physics, again, thermal equilibrium is characterized by diffusive, local, motion of particles.
elements.sent-2411-011: This translates into the slow mixing and relaxation dynamics of local reversible Markov chains, and it affects most Markov chains used in practice.
elements.sent-2411-012: In this paper, we appraise diametrically opposite strategies to overcome the slow diffusive motion of local, reversible MCMC methods.
elements.sent-2411-013: One is Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC), a non-local yet reversible Markov chain, and the other is event-chain Monte Carlo (ECMC), a class of lifted Markov chains, which are local yet non-reversible.
elements.sent-2411-014: HMC~\cite{DuKePeRo1987 emulates classical physical systems which, even when they are out of equilibrium, satisfy Newton's (or Hamilton's) equations.}
elements.sent-2411-015: In addition to their positions (the sample-space variables of the fictitious physical systems), particle systems have momenta.
elements.sent-2411-016: While the positions change in time as dictated by the momenta, momenta change with the gradient of a potential.
elements.sent-2411-017: Approaching equilibrium then corresponds to a gradient descent, which is generally assumed to be fast because it corresponds to a physical dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-018: In a nutshell, HMC introduces fictitious momenta and solves discretized Newton's equation to move from one configuration $x$ to a distant configuration $x'$.
elements.sent-2411-019: The time-discretization errors are eliminated through rejections which enforce detailed balance.
elements.sent-2411-020: ECMC~\cite{Bernard2009,Michel2014JCP introduces additional \quot{lifting variables which are more general than momenta.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: As the name indicates, ECMC is an event-driven implementation of a continuous-time (thus local) Markov process.
elements.sent-2411-022: It thus need not discretize time, and exactly satisfies global balance, a continuity condition which replaces detailed balance in the non-reversible case.
elements.sent-2411-023: The ECMC dynamics is neither diffusive nor gradient-based.
elements.sent-2411-024: In fact, it evaluates no global potential, potential differences or gradients, and it crucially depends on a parameter (a \quot{factor field ~\cite{Lei2019 or \quot{pullback ~\cite{Essler2024 ), which leaves the gradient invariant.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: For a many-particle harmonic chain on a one-dimensional ring that we use as a benchmark, ECMC has better scaling than HMC.
elements.sent-2411-026: In \SECT{sec:HarmonicThermodynamics of this paper, we review the harmonic chain~\cite{Lei2019 , compute its thermodynamics, and present basic sampling algorithms for it, on the one hand direct-sampling methods, and on the other the aforementioned local reversible Markov chains featuring slow diffusive dynamics.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: In \SECT{sec:HMC , we implement an HMC algorithm for the harmonic chain, following \REF{Neal2011 , and discuss the algorithm's stability and its relation to the molecular-dynamics method.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: In \SECT{sec:ECMC , we implement several variants of ECMC for the harmonic chain, following \REF{Lei2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: We discuss the essential link~\cite{Michel2014JCP between the behavior of the algorithm and the system pressure and outline how the algorithm has been generalized to more complex sampling problems.}
elements.sent-2411-030: In \SECT{sec:ComparisonsBenchmarks , we juxtapose the different sampling strategies and show how they effectively go beyond the diffusive regime and improve the scaling of autocorrelation times of global observables, in our case the structure factor.}
elements.sent-2411-031: In the concluding \SECT{sec:Conclusion we discuss the generic nature of the harmonic-chain dynamics both for HMC and ECMC.}
elements.sent-2411-032: We also discuss the relation of ECMC for the harmonic chain with a lattice model, the \quot{lifted TASEP (totally asymmetric simple exclusion model), whose dynamics can be analyzed exactly using the Bethe ansatz~\cite{Essler2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-033: The main text of this paper contains a number of pseudocode algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-034: The appendices provide mathematical details (\app{app:Math ), as well as further pseudocode algorithms and pointers towards an open-source repository containing all the Python programs discussed in this paper (\app{app:Algorithms ).}}
elements.section-2411-002: Harmonic chain: thermodynamics and basic sampling
elements.sent-2411-035: The harmonic chain describes particles on a continuous interval with periodic boundary conditions in space and in the indices.
elements.sent-2411-036: Each particle interacts with the two particles with neighboring indices.
elements.sent-2411-037: The interaction is harmonic with an equilibrium pair distance that drops out for the Metropolis algorithm and for HMC but is all important within ECMC.
elements.sent-2411-038: The harmonic chain is equivalent to the time-discretized path integral of a free quantum particle~\cite{SMAC .}
elements.sent-2411-039: In \SECT{subsec:DefinitionsThermodynamics , we define the harmonic-chain model and exactly compute its thermodynamics, in particular the pressure, whose vanishing defines a special point for ECMC.}
elements.sent-2411-040: We further discuss, in \SECT{subsec:Levy , direct-sampling algorithms for the harmonic chain and, in \SECT{subsec:GibbsMetropolis , examples of local reversible Markov chains featuring the slow dynamics that HMC and ECMC overcome in different ways.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: The Metropolis algorithm is the classic sampling approach, whereas the factorized Metropolis algorithm is the starting point for the nonreversible ECMC.
elements.sent-2411-042: The harmonic chain consists of $N$ particles $k \in \SET{0 \TO N-1}$ on a ring of length $L$ with periodic boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-043: For finite $L$, a convenient description is in terms of one periodic coordinate \begin{equation}0 \le x_0 < L, \intertext{and of elongations $x_k - x_{k-1}$ with } -\infty < x_1 \TO x_{N-1} < \infty. \intertext{Periodic boundary conditions are implemented through} x_N = x_0 + L . \label{equ:PeriodicN}\end{equation} Each configuration $x = \SET{x_0 \TO x_{N-1 }$ (with $x_N$ from \eq{equ:PeriodicN understood) has a harmonic potential \begin{equation}U(x, b, L) = \half \sum_{k=1}^{N} (x_k - x_{k-1} -b)^2\quad \label{equ:UofxB} = \half \sum_{k=1}^{N} (x_k - x_{k-1})^2 - b \sum_{k=1}^N (x_k - x_{k-1}) + \half N b^2 \label{equ:UofxFactorFields} = \half \sum_{k=1}^{N} (x_k - x_{k-1})^2 - b L + \half N b^2, \label{equ:UofxIndependent}\end{equation} with the \equidist.}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}0 \le x_0 < L, \intertext{and of elongations $x_k - x_{k-1}$ with } -\infty < x_1 \TO x_{N-1} < \infty. \intertext{Periodic boundary conditions are implemented through} x_N = x_0 + L . \label{equ:PeriodicN}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}U(x, b, L) = \half \sum_{k=1}^{N} (x_k - x_{k-1} -b)^2\quad \label{equ:UofxB} = \half \sum_{k=1}^{N} (x_k - x_{k-1})^2 - b \sum_{k=1}^N (x_k - x_{k-1}) + \half N b^2 \label{equ:UofxFactorFields} = \half \sum_{k=1}^{N} (x_k - x_{k-1})^2 - b L + \half N b^2, \label{equ:UofxIndependent}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-044: In \eq{equ:UofxIndependent , the terms in $b$ are the same for all particle positions, so that algorithms that rely on potential differences and gradients, such as HMC, are insensitive to them.}
elements.sent-2411-045: They play nevertheless a crucial role for ECMC dynamics~\cite{Lei2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-046: The Boltzmann weight of each configuration is given by \begin{equation}\pi(x, b, L) = \expc{- \beta U(x, b, L)}, \label{equ:Boltzmann}\end{equation} with the partition function \begin{equation}Z(N, b, L) = \int_{0}^{L} \diff x_0 \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \diff x_1 \dots \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \diff x_{N-1} \pi(x,b,L) = L \expc{\beta b L - \half \beta b^2 N - \half \beta L^2 N } \frac{2^{(N-1)/2}}{\sqrt{N}} \frac{\pi^{(N-1)/2}}{\beta^{(N-1)/2}} \label{equ:PartitionFunction}\end{equation} (see \app{app:Math for a derivation).}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}Z(N, b, L) = \int_{0}^{L} \diff x_0 \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \diff x_1 \dots \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \diff x_{N-1} \pi(x,b,L) = L \expc{\beta b L - \half \beta b^2 N - \half \beta L^2 N } \frac{2^{(N-1)/2}}{\sqrt{N}} \frac{\pi^{(N-1)/2}}{\beta^{(N-1)/2}} \label{equ:PartitionFunction}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-047: The pressure is \begin{equation}P|_{\beta = 1} = \partial \log Z / \partial L|_{\beta = 1} = \frac{1}{L} + b - \frac{L}{N}. \label{equ:FabulousFormula}\end{equation} For $b = \bcrit = L/N - 1/L$, the pressure vanishes.
elements.sent-2411-048: This defines a special point for ECMC.
elements.sent-2411-049: The mean energy is \begin{equation}\mean{U} |_{\beta = 1} = -\partial \log Z / \partial \beta |_{\beta = 1} = -bL + \half b^2 N + \half \frac{L^2}{N} + \frac{(N-1)}{2}. \label{equ:MeanEnergy}\end{equation} Its $b$-independent part (the last two terms on the right-hand side) will be used for testing our computer implementations.
elements.sent-2411-050: The gradient of the energy, \begin{equation}\nabla_k U(x, b, L) = \begin{cases} 2 x_0 - \glb x_{1} + x_{N-1} - L \grb \text{for $k=0$} 2 x_k - \glb x_{k+1} + x_{k-1} \grb \text{for $0< k< N-1$} 2 x_{N-1} - \glb x_{0} + L + x_{N-2} \grb \text{for $k = N-1$} \end{cases}, \label{equ:GradientHarmonic}\end{equation} is independent of $b$.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \newcommand{\algo}{levy-harmonic} \captionsetup{margin=0pt,justification=raggedright} \begin{center} $\begin{array}{ll} \PROCEDURE{\algo} \IS{x_0}{\ranb{0,L}} \IS{x_N}{x_0 + L} \FOR{k = 1 \TO N-1} \BRACE{ \IS{\xmean}{[(N-k) x_{k-1} + x_N]/(N-k+1)} \IS{\sigma}{\sqrt{1/[1 + 1/(N-k)]} } \IS{x_k}{\sub{gauss}(\xmean, \sigma) } } \OUTPUT{\SET{x_0 \TO x_{N-1}}}\ \COMMENT{configuration, sample of \eq{equ:Boltzmann}} \ENDPROCEDURE\ \end{array}$ \end{center} \caption{\sub{\algo}. Lévy construction (Gaussian bridge) providing a direct-sampling algorithm for the harmonic chain. This algorithm initializes all the Markov chains in this paper.} \label{alg:\algo}
elements.sent-2411-061: Any Markov chain that is irreducible and aperiodic converges to the distribution $\pi$ if it satisfies the global-balance condition~\cite{Levin2008 \begin{equation}\pi(x') = \sum_{x}\pi(x) P(x, x'). \label{equ:GlobalBalance}\end{equation} Here, the transition matrix $P(x, x')$ is the probability to move from a given point $x$ to $x'$ in one time step, from time $t$ to $t+1$, conditioned on the Markov chain being at position $x$ at time $t$.}
elements.sent-2411-062: A stricter condition for convergence is the detailed-balance condition \begin{equation}\pi(x) P(x, x') = \pi(x') P(x', x), \label{equ:DetailedBalance}\end{equation} which implies \eq{equ:GlobalBalance because of the conservation of probabilities $\sum_{x' P(x, x') = 1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Commonly, the transition matrix $P(x, x')$ is written as a product \begin{equation}P(x,x') = \ACAL(x,x') \PCAL(x, x').\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-064: Here, the a priori probability $\ACAL(x, x')$ proposes the move $x \to x'$, and the filter $\PCAL(x, x')$ accepts or rejects it.
elements.sent-2411-065: The Metropolis algorithm uses a symmetric $\ACAL$, \begin{equation}\ACAL(x, x') = \ACAL(x', x), \label{equ:APrioriSymmetric}\end{equation} with a specific choice of symmetric function: \begin{equation}\pi(x) \pMet(x, x') = \pi(x') \pMet(x', x) = \underbrace{\minc{\pi(x), \pi(x')}}_{\text{symmetric in $x$ and $x'$}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-066: As the right-hand side of this equation is symmetric in $x$ and $x'$, so must be the rest of the equation, so that detailed balance is satisfied.
elements.sent-2411-067: This leads to the famous Metropolis filter \begin{equation}\pMet(x, x') = \minc{1, \frac{\pi(x')}{\pi(x)}} = \mind{1, \expa{-\beta [U(x') - U(x)]}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-068: In the harmonic chain, for a move $x_k \to x_k '$ of a single particle $k$, the change of the potential $U$, written as in \eq{equ:UofxB , impacts two terms, namely \begin{equation}U_k^+ = \half (x^+ - x_k - b )^2;\quad U_k^- = \half (x_k - x^- -b)^2, \intertext{in the initial configuration containing $x_k$ and two terms, namely} U_k{'}^{+} = \half (x^+ - x_k' - b )^2;\quad U_k^- = \half (x_k' - x^- -b)^2,\end{equation} for the final configuration containing $x_k'$ (up to boundary conditions in space and in particle index, $x^+ = x_{k+1 $ and $x^- = x_{k-1 $).}}}

elements.section-2411-003: Hamiltonian Monte Carlo for the harmonic chain

elements.sent-2411-084: In \SECT{subsec:HMCAlgorithm , we implement HMC for the harmonic chain and sketch some of its basic properties.}

elements.sent-2411-087: HMC associates the position $x$ at a given time $t$ with a set of Gaussian momenta $p = \SET{p_1 \TO p_N}$.
elements.sent-2411-088: Together, they construct a probability distribution \begin{equation}\pi(x, p) = \expc{-\beta U(x)} \expb{ -\beta p^2/2}. \label{equ:UandK}\end{equation} The energy $U$ thus becomes the potential energy of a dynamical system with total energy $U + K$, where the kinetic energy is $K = \half p^2$.
elements.sent-2411-089: An ideal Hamiltonian time evolution moves in a time interval $\tau$ from $(x, p)$ to $(x', p')$.
elements.sent-2411-090: In the special case of the hard-sphere model, this dynamics samples the Boltzmann distribution rigorously~\cite{Sinai1970,Simanyi2003 .}
elements.sent-2411-091: In more general problems, the (ideal) Hamiltonian time evolution conserves the total energy $E = U + K$ but may not visit all configurations $(x,p)$ of energy $E$.

elements.subsection-2411-006: Relation of HMC to molecular dynamics
elements.sent-2411-114: HMC, as implemented in \prog{hmc-harmonic , contains three essential ingredients that are associated with distinct computations.}
elements.sent-2411-115: The first ingredient is the repeated evaluation of the gradient which iteratively time-steps the leapfrog algorithm from $x$ (and its associated momentum $p$) to $x'$ (and $p'$).
elements.sent-2411-116: In the harmonic chain, this step is insensitive to the parameter $b$, and its computational cost is \bigOb{N operations per time step (that is, per iteration $\iota$ in \progn{hmc-harmonic ).}}
elements.sent-2411-117: With long-range interactions, the evaluation of the gradient typically relies on fast Ewald methods~\cite{hockney1988ComputerSimulationUsingParticles or multipole expansions~\cite{Greengard1987 and cannot be performed efficiently to machine precision~\cite{Hoellmer2024 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-118: The second ingredient is the Metropolis filter that enforces the correct stationary distribution.
elements.sent-2411-119: It renders HMC reversible and rigorous.
elements.sent-2411-120: It evaluates the total-energy change between $(x,p)$ and $(\xtilde, \ptilde)$, and is again independent of $b$.
elements.sent-2411-121: In the general case, ECMC avoids the evaluation of $U$ or its derivatives yet still samples $\pi = \expb{-\beta U}$ (see \REF{Tartero2024b for an introduction).}
elements.sent-2411-122: The third ingredient is the refreshment (or thermalization) of the momenta---on the same time frame as the Metropolis step, which assures the irreducibility of the Markov chain.
elements.sent-2411-123: ECMC, as we will discuss later, also features auxiliary variables that are updated as the simulation proceeds, but never refreshed.
elements.sent-2411-124: With its rejection step, HMC samples the stationary distribution exactly.
elements.sent-2411-125: It is thus a rigorous variant of molecular dynamics, the hamiltonian time evolution coupled to a thermostat at all time steps $\epsilon$, but without a Metropolis decision.
elements.sent-2411-126: Such a thermostat hides instabilities (as shown in \fig{fig:HamiltonianAcceptanceProbability ), accumulated errors and energy drifts.}
elements.sent-2411-127: Thermostats thus may produce artifacts, but they often appear as a \quot{necessary evils ~\cite{Wong2016 .}}
elements.sent-2411-128: ECMC evaluates neither gradients not differences of the total energy and requires no refreshments and no thermostats.
elements.sent-2411-129: Its dynamics, however, claims no similarity with the physical dynamics.

elements.sent-2411-130: For the harmonic chain, ECMC~\cite{Bernard2009,Michel2014JCP corresponds to the factorized Metropolis algorithm of \progn{metropolis-factorized-harmonic (or its variants) with three changes.}}

elements.sent-2411-134: Second, moves are infinitesimal, resulting in a continuous-time Markov process.
elements.sent-2411-135: The rejection probability is so small (it is infinitesimal), that most moves are accepted.
elements.sent-2411-136: Any rejection can be traced to a specific factor, and in \progn{metropolis-factorized-harmonic either to the forward factor (the one involving $U_k^+$) or to the backward factor (the one involving $U_k^-$), but not to both.}

elements.sent-2411-145: We then focus on the role of the pressure of this algorithm.

elements.subsection-2411-011: Parameter dependence of HMC and ECMC
elements.sent-2411-190: For a configuration $x = \SET{x_0 \TO x_{N-1 }$ at time $t$, we define the structure factor as: \begin{equation}S(x,t) = \frac{1}{N} \gle \sum_{j=0}^{N-1}\expb{i q x_j}\gre ^2, \label{equ:StructureFactor}\end{equation} where $q = 2 \pi / L $ is the smallest non-zero wave number in a periodic system of length $L$.}
elements.sent-2411-191: The structure factor is sensitive to long-range density fluctuations, which are expected to relax slowly in equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-192: It is invariant under uniform translations of all particles, and it does not require to know particle indices or the connectivity matrix.
elements.sent-2411-193: As in earlier works, we use the autocorrelation time of the structure factor to benchmark MCMC algorithms, but a number of related observables give equivalent results.
elements.sent-2411-194: As discussed, HMC proposes non-local moves $x \to x'$ from the Newtonian evolution of an associated dynamical system.
elements.sent-2411-195: In the harmonic chain, these proposals are more efficient than those of the local Metropolis algorithm as, for $\epsilon \to 0$, essentially a single long move decorrelates the Markov chain.
elements.sent-2411-196: The length of this chain requires fine-tuning to values $\epsilon I \simeq 0.25N, 0.75N, \dots$ in order to avoid recurrence with $x \simeq x'$ (see \fig{fig:AutocorrelationsFixedN ).}
elements.sent-2411-197: For small non-zero step sizes $\epsilon$, the quality of the proposed moves degrades gracefully, and it remains true that a single long, accepted, move decorrelates the Markov chain.

elements.sent-2411-214: Reversible, local Markov chains form the backbone of the huge field of Monte Carlo methods.

elements.sent-2411-252: For the sake of completeness, we present two more sampling algorithms, one being a local Gibbs sampler, and the other the local reversible four-factor Metropolis algorithm, which serves as a starting point for the factor-field variant of ECMC (\prog{ecmc-fourfactor-harmonic ).}
elements.sent-2411-253: In a heat-bath algorithm (Gibbs sampler), a subsystem of the configuration $x$ is equilibrated with respect to its environment.
elements.sent-2411-254: The harmonic chain allows this subsystem to be the position of a single particle $k$ or any number of neigboring particles.
elements.sent-2411-255: This subsystem can be equilibrated through a variant of the Lévy construction, so that the Markov chain (implemented in \prog{gibbs-harmonic ) need not be local.}
elements.sent-2411-256: This contrasts with the situation for the local Metropolis algorithm, where the step-size $\delta$ and the number of displaced particles must be small in order to keep a sizeable acceptance rate.
elements.sent-2411-257: In general models, the heat-bath and Metropolis algorithms behave similarly.
elements.algorithm-2411-007: \newcommand{\algo}{gibbs-harmonic} \captionsetup{margin=0pt,justification=raggedright} \begin{center} $\begin{array}{ll} \PROCEDURE{\algo} }, t}\ \COMMENT{configuration, time} \IS{k}{\sub{choice}(\SET{0 \TO N-1})} \IS{k^+}{\sub{mod}(k+1,N)},\ \IS{k^-}{\sub{mod}(k-1,N)} \IS{x^+}{x_{k^+}},\ \IF{k = N-1}{\IS{x^+}{x^+ + L}} \IS{x^-}{x_{k^-}},\ \IF{k = 0}{\IS{x^-}{x^- - L}} \IS{\overline{x}}{\half( x^+ + x^-)},\ \IS{\sigma}{1/\sqrt{2}} \IS{x_k}{\sub{gauss}(\overline{x}, \sigma)}\ \COMMENT{compare to \prog{levy-harmonic}} \OUTPUT{\SET{x_0 \TO x_{N-1}}, t+1}\ \COMMENT{configuration, time} \ENDPROCEDURE\ \end{array}$ \end{center} \caption{\sub{\algo}. Heatbath Monte Carlo algorithm for the harmonic chain. The algorithm is independent of $b$.} \label{alg:\algo}

elements.algorithm-2411-008: \newcommand{\algo}{metropolis-fourfactor-harmonic} \captionsetup{margin=0pt,justification=raggedright} \begin{center} $\begin{array}{ll} \PROCEDURE{\algo} }, t}\ \COMMENT{configuration, time} \dots \COMMENT{$k,\Delta_x, k^+,k^-, x^+ , x^-, x_k' $ as in \progn{metropolis-harmonic}} \IS{U_k^+}{\half (x^+ - x_k)^2};\ \IS{U_k^-}{\half (x_k - x^-)^2} \IS{U_k^{\prime +} }{\half (x^+ - x_k')^2};\ \IS{U_k^{\prime -}}{\half (x_k' - x^-)^2} \IS{U_f^+}{-b (x^+ - x_k)};\ \IS{U_f^-}{ -b(x_k - x^-)} \IS{U_f^{\prime +}}{-b (x^+ - x_k')};\ \IS{U_f^{\prime -} }{ -b(x_k' - x^-)} \IS{\Upsilon^+_k}{\ranb{0,1}};\ \IS{\Upsilon^-_k}{\ranb{0,1}} \IS{\Upsilon^+_f}{\ranb{0,1}};\ \IS{\Upsilon^-_f}{\ranb{0,1}} * \IF{\Upsilon^+_k < \expc{-(U^{\prime +}_k- U^+_k)}\ \AND\ \Upsilon^-_k < \expc{-(U^{\prime -}_k - U^{-}_k)} \ \quad \AND\ \Upsilon^+_f < \expc{-(U^{\prime +}_f- U^+_f)}\ \AND\ \Upsilon^-_f < \expc{-(U^{\prime -}_f - U^{-}_f)}} \IS{x_k}{x'_k} \OUTPUT{\SET{x_0 \TO x_{N-1}}, t+1}\ \COMMENT{configuration, time} \ENDPROCEDURE\ \end{array}$ \end{center} \caption{\sub{\algo}. Alternative factorized Metropolis algorithm for the harmonic chain. The two-line condition * checks for the consensus of the four factors corresponding to \eqtwo{equ:FourTermsInitial}{equ:FourTermsFinal}. } \label{alg:\algo}
